did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for skin lesion classification from dermoscopic images. Specifically, it applied transfer learning using five CNN architectures (ResNet-101, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet, VGG16, and MobileNetV2) on the HAM10000-1 dataset to classify seven types of skin lesions. DenseNet-121 achieved the best results with 87% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of transfer learning with five CNN architectures (ResNet-101, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet, VGG16, and MobileNetV2) on the HAM10000-1 dataset for skin lesion classification. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compares the results of these new experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Lesions From Dermoscopic Images Using Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: 2023 24th International Conference on Control Systems and Computer Science (CSCS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin lesions from dermoscopic images to assist in early melanoma detection. The authors applied transfer learning using five Convolutional Neural Network architectures: ResNet-101, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet, VGG16, and MobileNetV2. The experiments were conducted on the HAM10000-1 dataset, which was created by augmenting the original HAM10000 dataset to balance the seven diagnostic classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. The DenseNet-121 architecture achieved the best overall results with 87% accuracy, 0.871 precision, 0.87 recall, and 0.872 F1-score.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two malignant lesion classes in the HAM10000-1 dataset used for seven-class classification.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic classes of skin lesions (MEL, BCC, AK/IEC, NV, BKL, DF, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs seven-class classification of skin lesions from dermoscopic images using five CNN architectures (ResNet-101, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet, VGG16, and MobileNetV2) on the HAM10000-1 dataset. The task involves assigning discrete diagnostic labels to images, evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000-1
datasets_used.description: A balanced dataset derived from the original HAM10000 dataset through image augmentation and selection to address class imbalance. It contains dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions collected from different populations.

use.training: Used to train five CNN architectures (ResNet-101, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet, VGG16, and MobileNetV2) via transfer learning.
use.testing: Used to evaluate and compare the performance of the five CNN architectures using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The original unbalanced dataset containing 10,015 RGB images with a resolution of 450x600 pixels, from which HAM10000-1 was derived.

use.training: Source dataset for creating the balanced HAM10000-1 dataset via augmentation.
use.other: Referenced as the source of the original data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000-1.experiment: Seven-class classification using transfer learning with five CNN architectures
HAM10000-1.notes: DenseNet-121 achieved the best overall results for the seven-class classification. The class with the best accuracy values across all architectures was VASC, while the class with the lowest values was NV. The difference between training and testing results was not very large, indicating the models did not overfit.

architecture_used.main_model: Five CNN architectures: ResNet-101, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet (Inception v1), VGG16, and MobileNetV2
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset; transfer learning applied by modifying the last layer(s) to output seven classes instead of 1000
architecture_used.input_size: 450x600 pixels (original HAM10000 images); images were augmented via cropping, rotation (90, 180, 270 degrees), and horizontal flipping

architecture_details.ResNet-101: Deep Residual Network; won ImageNet challenge in 2015
architecture_details.DenseNet-121: Dense Network variant with 121 layers; uses dense blocks with consecutive operations (Batch Normalization, ReLU, 3x3 convolution) and transition layers (1x1 convolution, 2x2 average pooling)
architecture_details.GoogLeNet: Also called Inception v1; winner of 2014 ILSVRC; features 1x1 convolutions, global average pooling, Inception modules (parallel 1x1, 3x3, 5x5 convolutions and 3x3 max-pooling), and auxiliary classifier
architecture_details.VGG16: Visual Geometry Group architecture; simple and efficient design
architecture_details.MobileNetV2: Improved version of MobileNetV1; includes 17 bottleneck blocks (expansion module, depth-wise separable convolution, point-wise convolution) and residual connections

training_details.hardware: AMD Ryzen 7 5700G processor, 16 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 12 GB GPU
training_details.data_split: 70% training (5126 images), 10% validation (569 images), 20% testing (1425 images)

classification_or_output_head.modification: Last layer(s) modified from 1000 classes (ImageNet) to 7 classes

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using the DullRazor algorithm to eliminate artifacts and improve lesion visibility
Image augmentation including cropping, rotation (90, 180, 270 degrees), and horizontal flipping to balance the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images as part of the augmentation process to create the balanced HAM10000-1 dataset.
Images were rotated by 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
The horizontally flipped images were also rotated (implied as part of the augmentation operations implemented).

conclusions: DenseNet-121 achieved the best overall performance for seven-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000-1 dataset, with 87% accuracy, 0.871 precision, 0.87 recall, and 0.872 F1-score.
The difference between training and testing results was small across all architectures, indicating that the models did not overfit and were able to generalize well.
The VASC (Vascular Lesion) class achieved the highest accuracy values across all tested architectures, while the NV (Melanocytic Nevus) class had the lowest values.
GoogLeNet achieved the best results for the MEL (Melanoma) class with an F1-score of 0.82 and for the BCC (Basal Cell Carcinoma) class with an F1-score of 0.91.
DenseNet-121 obtained the best F1-scores for the NV (0.74), AK/IEC (0.93), BKL (0.76), DF (0.99), and VASC (0.99) classes.
The study demonstrates that transfer learning with CNN architectures can effectively assist dermatologists in the diagnosis process by improving the accuracy of skin lesion classification.
important_remarks: The study uses a custom balanced dataset (HAM10000-1) created via augmentation and selection from the original HAM10000 dataset, rather than the standard, unbalanced HAM10000 split, which limits direct comparability with other studies using the standard benchmark.
The dataset was derived from images collected from different populations and acquired using various modalities, introducing potential heterogeneity in image quality and acquisition artifacts.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the five CNN architectures.

Performance varies significantly by class, with VASC (Vascular Lesion) achieving the highest accuracy (up to 100%) and NV (Melanocytic Nevus) the lowest (73.4%), indicating potential bias or difficulty in distinguishing specific benign lesions.
The study is limited to seven diagnostic classes, excluding other potential skin conditions or subtypes not present in the HAM10000 dataset.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the testing split of the derived HAM10000-1 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to unseen clinical data.

The paper does not report computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time) or model size, which are critical for assessing the feasibility of deploying these models in resource-constrained clinical settings.

Data leakage risk exists if the augmentation process (cropping, rotation, flipping) was not strictly confined to the training set during the creation of HAM10000-1, although the paper states it was created before the train/test split.
